scenes.title: Programs are values
scenes.subtitle: Expressions, not statements
lines: FunctionDefinition
cheerful
Welcome to Wordplay! I hear you know a bit about programming? Great! This brief tour shows what's similar and what's different about Wordplay.

Just so you know everything on this screen, this page shows @tutorial dialog and a @project view. You can see the *stage* @UI/stage, which shows program output, and *editor* @UI/editor, which is the program code.
This program is blank, so it's output is just a prompt to write a program.
There are many other controls for pausing and playing the program and editing code, but don't worry about those for now.
FunctionDefinition
happy
In most programming languages, a program is a list of statements that tell the computer what to *do*, like printing a @value:
\py|print("hi")\js|console.log('hi')\jv|System.out.println("hi");\
In Wordplay, programs are *expressions* that say what the /result/ of the program should be, not how it should produce it.
This program evalutes to the value \'hi'\, which is then shown on stage. No print command, just an @expression that says what value to evaluate to.
Bind
neutral
In other languages, a *variable* is a named container for a @value, and its value can change:
In Wordplay, named values can never change. They're 'immutable', meaning that they cannot be reassigned new values. That's why we call them a @Bind instead of a @variable, as they cannot vary.
We talk soon about how to write interactive programs without variables that can change.

scenes.title: Checking conditions
lines: Conditional
neutral
Other programming languages typically have an *if* statement to do different things based on a @condition:
In Wordplay, everything is an @expression, so the @Conditional is an *expression* written with a condition, then a \?\, then a true expression and a false expression. No keywords required
FunctionDefinition
curious
Some languages also have a *switch* statement to do different things based on a @value:
Wordplay has @Match, which picks a result by value.
FunctionDefinition
excited
Some languages also have a *null coalescing* @operator to provide a default @value when a @variable is null or undefined:
Wordplay has the @Otherwise operator to achieve the same result.

scenes.title: Values and types
lines: FunctionDefinition
neutral
Most languages have many 'primitive' @value types.
Wordplay has a similar syntax for these types, but also allows you to add other information, such as @language tags for multilingual text and units on numbers.
FunctionDefinition
excited
Programming languages also have ways of creating collections of values, like lists, sets, and maps (often known as dictionaries or hashes):
Wordplay uses a different syntax for these collections, most notably using spaces to separate values instead of commas.
FunctionDefinition
curious
Some programming languages allow you to specify the @type of a @variable, so you can ensure all the ways you use the variable are valid:

\py| list: list[int] = [1, 2, 3]\js| // JavaScript doesn't have type annotations. TypeScript does though:
let list: number[] = [1, 2, 3];\jv| List<Integer> list = Arrays.asList(1, 2, 3);\
Wordplay is typed too, but types are *inferred*. You usually don't need to declare them, but sometimes you might have to if they can't be inferred.
FunctionDefinition
curious
Programming languages usually offer a way to make custom data types too, to combine multiple values into a single data structure:
Wordplay uses a different syntax to declare a @Structure, using \•\ to represent a data type, then a @name, then the fields in the structure.

scenes.title: Conversions, collections, and functions
lines: FunctionDefinition
curious
A common operation in programming is to convert data from one @type to another. For example, we might want to convert a number to a text @value:
\py| str(1)\js| String(1)\jv| String.valueOf(1)\

Wordplay values all have a @Convert construct that allows you to convert between types. This example converts \1m\ to feet, and then to @Text. Each type of value has a whole collection of supported conversions
FunctionDefinition
neutral
For collections of values like lists and arrays, other programming languages use constructs like *for* and *while* loops to construct a new collection from an existing collection's values.

Wordplay's has a simple @Translate* construct that uses the \↦\ symbol: it builds a new @List, @Set or @Map, replacing each item (represented by the \⬚\ @placeholder), with a new @value. This doubles each item to make \[2 4 6]\.
There are also more powerful things like @List.translate and @List.combine, in case you need more complex operations.
FunctionDefinition
happy
Every programming language has functions, which allow to you declare reusable @blocks of code that take input and produce output based on them, of arbtirary complexity:
Wordplay also supports function definitions, but with the ƒ symbol. Just like all Wordplay programs, functions must evaluate to a @value.

scenes.title: Output is typographic, input is reactive

lines.edit: Stage([Phrase('🎉' resting:Sequence({0%: Pose(scale: 1) 50%: Pose(scale: 1.5) 100%: Pose(scale: 1)} duration: 2s))] background: Color(90% 0 0°))

lines: Stage
neutral
Other programming languages print text to a console, draw to a canvas, or modify a webpage:
\py| print('hi')\js| document.body.append('hi')\jv| System.out.println("hi");\
Wordplay's output is typographic: a @Phrase, a @Group, or a @Stage, which shows styled, animated, and interactive text on screen, or read by a screen reader.
The example below shows a @Stage with a single @Phrase that has a resting scaling animation.
FunctionDefinition
neutral
Most programming languages have a way to take input from the user:

\py| name = input('What is your name?')\js| const name = prompt('What is your name?')\jv| Scanner scanner = new Scanner(System.in);
String name = scanner.nextLine();\
Wordplay's input comes from *streams*, which are special values that change over time, in response to input, and cause the Wordplay program to re-evaluate with the stream's new value.

This program, for example, uses a @Time stream to drive the rotation of the globe emoji.Every time the @Time() stream ticks to a new @value, the program re-evaluates with the new value, updating the value of the program on @Stage.
FunctionDefinition
curious
Interactivity in most programming languages elsewhere means registering event handlers and callbacks:

In Wordplay, interactivity we can just use streams like @Button, @Key, and @Pointer to create interactive elements. The text of this @Phrase is based on whether the @Button is clicked, using a @Conditional to see if the @Button stream has @Changed.
FunctionDefinition
happy

Other programming languages let you change the values of variables at runtime, which can be useful for creating dynamic and interactive applications. But they require you to remember to update the values in the correct places, and update what is shown on screen when those values change:

Wordplay simplifies this by using a @Reaction @expression, which starts at an initial value, checks a @condition that refers to stream inputs, and updates the value of the expression based on the stream's changes. In this example, every time the @Button stream changes, we update the value of count, and that updates the @Phrase shown on @Stage.

scenes.title: Conflicts and debugging
lines: FunctionDefinition
angry
Other languages throw *errors* — a sign a program did something that's not allowed:
\py|1 + 'hello'\js|1 + 'hello'\jv|1 + "hello"\
Wordplay calls errors *conflicts*: two things that can't both be true, like adding a number and text in the program below.
Wordplay will underline conflicts and show them in the sidebar @UI/conflict. It will also offer conflict resolution suggestions, to fix the problem.
FunctionDefinition
happy

Most programming languages offer some kind of debugging tools to help creators understand and resolve issues in their code. But many just use print statements to check the values of variables in a console:
\py|print(elapsed)\js|console.log(elapsed)\jv|System.out.println(elapsed)\

Wordplay provides a more sophisticated debugging experience, allowing you to step *fowards* and *backwards* through its evaluation. Press the ⏸️ step button in the mode switcher @UI/modeSwitcher above the stage to enter *step* mode: a timeline appears below the output, the value of each expression appears next to it in the code, and the @UI/stepControls step evaluation in any direction, so you can watch how a value is built.
The other modes matter too: ✏️ *edit* mode is for changing the program while the stage stays frozen, and ▶️ *play* mode runs it live, responding to inputs in real time.
Want more guidance? The @UI/stageTourLaunch button next to the stage's name gives a guided tour of these controls.

scenes.title: Putting it together
lines: FunctionDefinition
happy
Let's build a tiny interactive @project in three steps.
*One:* declare your data and *streams*. Here a @Time stream named elapsed reports how long the program has run.
FunctionDefinition
neutral
*Two:* *react* to the stream by deriving new @Bind values. Here turn converts the elapsed time into degrees, recomputing whenever elapsed changes.
FunctionDefinition
happy
*Three:* *output* — map your data to a @Phrase, @Group, or @Stage. Now the star spins on its own!
FunctionDefinition
happy
We can make this even more interactive by adding user input to control the rotation, like the @Volume of microphone input. It's a value between 0 and 1, so we can map it on onto 360 degrees.
That's the shape of most projects: declarations, then reactions, then output. The <Guide@://guide> has a @how section full of recipes to study, copy, and edit.
Happy creating!
